origin: A short passage from an ancient Christian scripture, specifically a genealogy listing ancestral names (Σαλμὼν → Βόες → Ἰωβὴδ → Ἰεσσαί), matching the lineage section in the Gospel of Matthew (Jesus’ ancestry), using Greek (Koine) biblical language.
author: Most likely the Gospel writer/traditional attribution of Matthew, or more broadly an early Christian scribe/translator preserving Matthew’s Greek text (not a modern individual author).
thoughts: The authorial perspective is largely documentary and genealogy-focused—presenting names in a matter-of-fact chain. The motivation is likely apologetic/theological: to connect the story of Jesus to key biblical figures (Rahab, Ruth, and ultimately Jesse/David), reinforcing claims about fulfillment of prophecy and rightful lineage.
audience: Early Christian and/or Jewish(-background) readers who care about biblical continuity and genealogical proof—people interested in how Jesus relates to Israel’s sacred history and Davidic descent.
written_when: Late 1st century CE, commonly dated to roughly ~80–90 CE for Matthew’s composition (with the Greek wording reflecting that early textual tradition).
